insights.insight: The protocol settles verified purchase receipts at ~$0.10 each versus $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a pricing negotiation, it's a different category of evidence entirely.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the ad industry has no cross-ecosystem, identity-free mechanism to confirm a purchase actually happened — this dataset produces exactly that signal at 3,827 verified receipts and counting.

insights.topic: Physical retail dominance in the dataset
insights.insight: As of the latest update, grocery, big-box, and discount chains (Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Dollar General) now dominate the receipt pool, meaning the verified signal covers real-world basket behavior — the hardest and most valuable data to source outside a retailer's own walls.

Amazon knows Amazon, Walmart knows Walmart, banks see transaction amounts but not SKUs — no single platform sees the full purchase picture; this protocol's identity-free aggregate layer is the first cross-retailer view not owned by any one of those platforms.

With 23 active metro areas including rural and non-metro states like Kentucky, Maine, and Wisconsin, the dataset is spreading beyond urban early-adopter populations — which means it's beginning to reflect actual national purchasing behavior, not a coastal tech sample.

Fetch and Ibotta hold real purchase data but it is identity-attached, locked to their platforms, and not queryable by third-party brands — the structural difference here is cryptographically verified receipts that are cross-ecosystem and not tied to a consumer identity record.
